content:
# Genesis Chapter 39 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 22v–23r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_039_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_039_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 37–38 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0041 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0042

---

*Joseph Brought Down to Egypt — The LORD with Joseph (vv. 1–6a)*

**39:1** And Joseph was brought down [וְיוֹסֵף הוּרַד] to Egypt [מִצְרָיְמָה] — and Potiphar [וַיִּקְנֵהוּ פּוֹטִיפַר] — a court official [סְרִיס] of Pharaoh [פַּרְעֹה] — chief of the guard [שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים] — an Egyptian man [אִישׁ מִצְרִי] — bought him [וַיִּקְנֵהוּ] from the hand of the Ishmaelites [מִיַּד הַיִּשְׁמְעֵאלִים] who had brought him down there [אֲשֶׁר הוֹרִדֻהוּ שָׁמָּה].

**39:2** And the LORD was with Joseph [וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת-יוֹסֵף] — and he was a successful man [וַיְהִי אִישׁ מַצְלִיחַ] — and he was in the house [וַיְהִי בְּבֵית] of his master the Egyptian [אֲדֹנָיו הַמִּצְרִי].

**39:3** And his master saw [וַיַּרְא אֲדֹנָיו] that the LORD was with him [כִּי יְהוָה אִתּוֹ] — and that everything he did [וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר-הוּא עֹשֶׂה] the LORD was making prosper [יְהוָה מַצְלִיחַ] in his hand [בְּיָדוֹ].

**39:4** And Joseph found favor [וַיִּמְצָא יוֹסֵף חֵן] in his eyes [בְּעֵינָיו] — and he served him [וַיְשָׁרֶת אֹתוֹ] — and he appointed him [וַיַּפְקִדֵהוּ] over his house [עַל-בֵּיתוֹ] — and all that he had [וְכָל-יֶשׁ-לוֹ] he put into his hand [נָתַן בְּיָדוֹ].

**39:5** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] from the time he appointed him [מֵאָז הִפְקִיד אֹתוֹ] over his house [בְּבֵיתוֹ] and over all that he had [וְעַל כָּל-אֲשֶׁר יֶשׁ-לוֹ] — that the LORD blessed [וַיְבָרֶךְ יְהוָה] the house of the Egyptian [אֶת-בֵּית הַמִּצְרִי] on account of Joseph [בִּגְלַל יוֹסֵף] — and the blessing of the LORD was [וַיְהִי בִּרְכַּת יְהוָה] upon all that he had [בְּכָל-אֲשֶׁר יֶשׁ-לוֹ] — in the house [בַּבַּיִת] and in the field [וּבַשָּׂדֶה].

**39:6** And he left [וַיַּעֲזֹב] all that he had [כָּל-אֲשֶׁר לוֹ] in the hand of Joseph [בְּיַד-יוֹסֵף] — and he did not concern himself with anything at all [וְלֹא-יָדַע אִתּוֹ מְאוּמָה] except for the bread that he ate [כִּי אִם-הַלֶּחֶם אֲשֶׁר-הוּא אוֹכֵל] — and Joseph was [וַיְהִי יוֹסֵף] beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance [יְפֵה-תֹאַר וִיפֵה מַרְאֶה].

---

*The Demand of Potiphar's Wife — Joseph's Refusal (vv. 6b–10)*

**39:7** And it came to pass after these things [וַיְהִי אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה] — and the wife of his master [וַתִּשָּׂא אֵשֶׁת-אֲדֹנָיו] lifted up her eyes [אֶת-עֵינֶיהָ] toward Joseph [אֶל-יוֹסֵף] — and she said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: Lie with me [שִׁכְבָה עִמִּי].

**39:8** And he refused [וַיְמָאֵן] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to the wife of his master [אֶל-אֵשֶׁת אֲדֹנָיו]: Behold, my master [הֵן אֲדֹנִי] does not concern himself with me [לֹא-יָדַע אִתִּי] about anything in the house [מַה-בַּבַּיִת] — and all that he has [וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר-יֶשׁ-לוֹ] he has put into my hand [נָתַן בְּיָדִי].

**39:9** There is none greater [אֵינֶנּוּ גָדוֹל] in this house than I [בַּבַּיִת הַזֶּה מִמֶּנִּי] — and he has withheld nothing from me [וְלֹא-חָשַׂךְ מִמֶּנִּי מְאוּמָה] except for you [כִּי אִם-אוֹתָךְ] — because you are his wife [בַּאֲשֶׁר אַתְּ-אִשְׁתּוֹ] — and how shall I do [וְאֵיךְ אֶעֱשֶׂה] this great evil [הָרָעָה הַגְּדֹלָה הַזֹּאת] and sin against God [וְחָטָאתִי לֵאלֹהִים]?

**39:10** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] as she spoke to Joseph [כְּדַבְּרָהּ אֶל-יוֹסֵף] day after day [יוֹם יוֹם] — and he did not listen to her [וְלֹא-שָׁמַע אֵלֶיהָ] — to lie beside her [לִשְׁכַּב אֶצְלָהּ] — to be with her [לִהְיוֹת עִמָּהּ].

---

*The Garment Seized — The Flight (vv. 11–12)*

**39:11** And it came to pass on such a day [וַיְהִי כְּהַיּוֹם הַזֶּה] — and he came into the house [וַיָּבֹא הַבַּיְתָה] to do his work [לַעֲשׂוֹת מְלַאכְתּוֹ] — and there was none of the men of the house [וְאֵין אִישׁ מֵאַנְשֵׁי הַבַּיִת] there in the house [שָׁם בַּבָּיִת].

**39:12** And she seized him [וַתִּתְפְּשֵׂהוּ] by his garment [בְּבִגְדוֹ] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Lie with me [שִׁכְבָה עִמִּי] — and he left his garment [וַיַּעֲזֹב בִּגְדוֹ] in her hand [בְּיָדָהּ] — and he fled [וַיָּנָס] and went outside [וַיֵּצֵא הַחוּצָה].

---

*The False Accusation — Potiphar's Anger (vv. 13–20)*

**39:13** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] when she saw [כִּרְאוֹתָהּ] that he had left his garment [כִּי-עָזַב בִּגְדוֹ] in her hand [בְּיָדָהּ] — and had fled outside [וַיָּנָס הַחוּצָה].

**39:14** And she called [וַתִּקְרָא] to the men of her house [לְאַנְשֵׁי בֵיתָהּ] and spoke to them [וַתְּדַבֵּר אֲלֵיהֶם] saying [לֵאמֹר]: See [רְאוּ] — he brought to us a Hebrew man [הֵבִיא לָנוּ אִישׁ עִבְרִי] to make sport of us [לְצַחֶק בָּנוּ] — he came to me [בָּא אֵלַי] to lie with me [לִשְׁכַּב עִמִּי] — and I called out [וָאֶקְרָא] in a great voice [בְּקוֹל גָּדוֹל].

**39:15** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] when he heard [כְּשָׁמְעוֹ] that I raised my voice [כִּי-הֲרִימֹתִי קוֹלִי] and called out [וָאֶקְרָא] — that he left his garment [וַיַּעֲזֹב בִּגְדוֹ] beside me [אֶצְלִי] — and he fled [וַיָּנָס] and went outside [וַיֵּצֵא הַחוּצָה].

**39:16** And she kept his garment [וַתַּנַּח בִּגְדוֹ אֶצְלָהּ] beside her [אֶצְלָהּ] — until his master came [עַד-בּוֹא אֲדֹנָיו] to his house [אֶל-בֵּיתוֹ].

**39:17** And she spoke to him [וַתְּדַבֵּר אֵלָיו] according to these words [כַּדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה] saying [לֵאמֹר]: The Hebrew slave [בָּא-אֵלַי הָעֶבֶד הָעִבְרִי] — the one you brought to us [אֲשֶׁר-הֵבֵאתָ לָּנוּ] — came to me [בָּא אֵלַי] to make sport of me [לְצַחֶק בִּי].

**39:18** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] when I raised my voice [כַּהֲרִימִי קוֹלִי] and called out [וָאֶקְרָא] — that he left his garment [וַיַּעֲזֹב בִּגְדוֹ] beside me [אֶצְלִי] — and he fled outside [וַיָּנָס הַחוּצָה].

**39:19** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] when his master heard [כִּשְׁמֹעַ אֲדֹנָיו] the words of his wife [אֶת-דִּבְרֵי אִשְׁתּוֹ] that she spoke to him [אֲשֶׁר דִּבְּרָה אֵלָיו] saying [לֵאמֹר]: According to these words [כַּדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה] your slave did to me [עָשָׂה-לִי עַבְדֶּךָ] — and his anger burned [וַיִּחַר אַפּוֹ].

**39:20** And the master of Joseph took him [וַיִּקַּח אֲדֹנֵי יוֹסֵף אֹתוֹ] — and he put him [וַיִּתְּנֵהוּ] into the prison-house [אֶל-בֵּית הַסֹּהַר] — the place where the king's prisoners were bound [מְקוֹם אֲשֶׁר-אֲסִירֵי הַמֶּלֶךְ אֲסוּרִים] — and he was there [וַיְהִי-שָׁם] in the prison-house [בְּבֵית הַסֹּהַר].

---

*The LORD with Joseph in Prison — He Prospers Again (vv. 21–23)*

**39:21** And the LORD was with Joseph [וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת-יוֹסֵף] — and he extended covenant-faithfulness to him [וַיֵּט אֵלָיו חָסֶד] — and he gave his favor [וַיִּתֵּן חִנּוֹ] in the eyes of the warden of the prison-house [בְּעֵינֵי שַׂר בֵּית-הַסֹּהַר].

**39:22** And the warden of the prison-house put [וַיִּתֵּן שַׂר בֵּית-הַסֹּהַר] into the hand of Joseph [בְּיַד-יוֹסֵף] all the prisoners [אֶת-כָּל-הָאֲסִירִים] who were in the prison-house [אֲשֶׁר בְּבֵית הַסֹּהַר] — and all that they did there [וְאֵת כָּל-אֲשֶׁר עֹשִׂים שָׁם] — he was the one doing it [הוּא הָיָה עֹשֶׂה].

**39:23** The warden of the prison-house was not looking at [אֵין שַׂר בֵּית-הַסֹּהַר רֹאֶה] anything at all [אֶת-כָּל-מְאוּמָה] under his hand [בְּיָדוֹ] — in as much as [בַּאֲשֶׁר] the LORD was with him [יְהוָה אִתּוֹ] — and that which he did [וַאֲשֶׁר-הוּא עֹשֶׂה] the LORD was making prosper [יְהוָה מַצְלִיחַ].

---

## Textual and Philological Notes — Genesis 39

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 22v–23r**
The chapter begins partway down the right-hand column of folio 22v (page 43), immediately following the close of Genesis 38. The chapter setumah marker (ס) is visible between 38:30 and 39:1. The opening verse begins with the waw-consecutive וְיוֹסֵף הוּרַד — the waw on Joseph's name serving as a deliberate verbal hinge linking back to 37:36 (וְיוֹסֵף הוּרַד מִצְרָיְמָה) where the same clause appears verbatim, completing the narrative loop interrupted by Genesis 38. The Masoretic pointing is clear throughout. Chapter 39 runs continuously through folio 23r (page 44) and is complete by approximately the midpoint of that folio. The masora parva marks several key terms including the double yefeh construction in verse 6 and the phrase chesed in verse 21. No lacunae are present. The consonantal text is fully pointed with Ben Asher vocalization. The verse divisions are standard and agree with BHS.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 37–38**
Page 37 (pa_0037) carries Genesis 38–39. The chapter marker for Genesis 39 (CAP XLIII visible at the right-column midpoint of page 37 — the Vaticanus chapter enumeration runs differently from the modern chapter count, and the marker XLIII at this location on p. 37 corresponds to approximately the transition point from chapter 38 to 39 material in the LXX text column) begins the Joseph-in-Egypt section. Page 38 (pa_0038) continues with the latter portion of Genesis 39 and introduces Genesis 40. The LXX text in Vaticanus is written in three columns in the characteristic 4th-century uncial hand, with occasional ligatures. The text of Genesis 39 in Vaticanus is complete with no significant lacunae. Several notable variants from the MT are documented below in the LXX variant table.

---

### Verse-by-Verse Notes

**v. 1 — hurad (הוּרַד) — "was brought down" — the Hophal passive**
The Hebrew verb form hurad is a Hophal (passive of the Hiphil) of the root yarad (ירד, "to go down"). The Hophal passive is the most passive form in the Hebrew verbal system — Joseph is not merely going down; he is being brought down against his will, by external agents. The Hiphil had appeared in 37:25 (the Ishmaelites "brought him down" to Egypt) and in 37:35 (Jacob refused "to go down" in mourning). Now the chapter-opening Hophal frames the entire Joseph story as one of God's working through involuntary circumstances.

This Hophal is the grammatical counterpart to the active yarad of Genesis 38:1 (וַיֵּרֶד יְהוּדָה — "and Judah went down"). Judah descends actively and voluntarily; Joseph is brought down passively. The narrative contrast between the two brothers' descents — one moral, one physical-geographical — is embedded in the grammar. The verbal root recurs within verse 1 itself: "the Ishmaelites who had brought him down there" (אֲשֶׁר הוֹרִדֻהוּ שָׁמָּה) — a Hiphil plural form, making clear that it was the Ishmaelites, not Joseph, who moved him downward.

The LXX renders: Ιωσηφ δὲ κατήχθη εἰς Αἴγυπτον ("And Joseph was brought down into Egypt") — using the aorist passive κατήχθη (from κατάγω, "to bring down"), precisely capturing the Hophal passive sense. This is one of the more accurate LXX renderings in the chapter.

**v. 1 — seris Paroh (סְרִיס פַּרְעֹה) — "a court official of Pharaoh"**
The term seris (סְרִיס) has a range of meanings. In later Hebrew and in cognate Semitic languages (Akkadian: ša rēši, "he of the head") it denotes a royal court official. The term can mean "eunuch" in the physiological sense, but it is frequently used simply for a high court officer without implying castration (cf. Potiphar's wife narrative — he is married — and 1 Sam 8:15; 2 Kings 8:6; 25:19). Many commentators argue that "court official" is the correct rendering here given Potiphar's married status. The LXX renders: σπάδοντι Φαραω (σπάδων = eunuch/castrated), choosing the physiological meaning — a significant interpretive choice, though it creates narrative tension with the fact that Potiphar has a wife. The Targum Onkelos similarly renders rvbt' (rabba) = "chief officer," avoiding the castration sense.

**v. 1 — sar hatabachim (שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים) — "chief of the guard"**
The title sar hatabachim is literally "chief of the slaughterers/butchers" (tabbach from the root tabach, "to slaughter"). In Egyptian administrative context this title appears to correspond to the head of the royal bodyguard or the captain of the royal executioners — the men responsible for executing the king's sentences. The Akkadian cognate term is used similarly. The LXX renders: ἀρχιμαγείρῳ ("chief cook"), preserving the culinary literal sense of tabbach. The Vulgate renders praeposito militum ("commander of soldiers"), reading the administrative military sense. The BHS notation and most modern translations render "captain/chief of the guard" as the best approximation of the Egyptian administrative role.

**v. 2 — ish matsliach (אִישׁ מַצְלִיחַ) — "a successful man"**
The root tsalach (צָלַח, Hiphil: hitsliyach) means "to prosper, to succeed, to make progress." The Hiphil participle matsliach ("causing to prosper" / "prosperous") describes Joseph's condition as flowing from the LORD's presence, not from his own merit alone. The phrase appears three times in the chapter: v. 2 (matsliach — he was a prosperous man), v. 3 (matzliach — the LORD was making prosper), and v. 23 (matzliach — the LORD was making prosper). The bracketing of the chapter by this root (prosperity in Potiphar's house = vv. 2–3; prosperity in the prison = v. 23) forms an inclusio: no matter how far Joseph descends, the LORD's making-prosper follows him. This triple usage is the structural backbone of the chapter's theology.

**v. 2 — "the LORD was with Joseph" (וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת-יוֹסֵף)**
The divine presence formula appears four times in the chapter (vv. 2, 3, 21, 23), always with the Tetragrammaton (YHWH, rendered "the LORD"). The formula "YHWH was with X" occurs in the patriarchal narratives for Isaac (26:24), Jacob (28:15; 31:3), and now Joseph — it is the covenant formula of divine accompaniment. The fact that YHWH's name is used, not Elohim, is significant: in the Joseph narrative the narrator uses YHWH in these presence-formulas (ch. 39 — the only chapter in the Joseph story where YHWH appears by name in narrative prose) while the human characters in the story use Elohim (v. 9: "sin against God/Elohim"). This distinction may reflect the narrator's inside theological perspective versus the characters' speech.

**v. 3 — "his master saw" — ki YHWH ito (כִּי יְהוָה אִתּוֹ)**
That an Egyptian master perceives the LORD's presence with his Hebrew slave is one of the most remarkable theological statements in Genesis. Potiphar — a pagan official — recognizes that something supernatural is accompanying Joseph. The basis of this perception is the observable evidence: "everything he did the LORD was making prosper in his hand" (v. 3b). The LORD's blessing is materially visible — it creates a track record legible even to one who does not know YHWH by covenant name. This anticipates the later pattern in the Joseph story where Pharaoh himself will recognize the spirit of Elohim in Joseph (41:38).

**v. 6 — yefeh-toar vi-yefeh mareh (יְפֵה-תֹאַר וִיפֵה מַרְאֶה) — "beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance"**
This double beauty formula — to'ar (תֹּאַר, "form, outline, shape") and mareh (מַרְאֶה, "appearance, sight") — is the identical phrase used of Rachel in Genesis 29:17: "and Rachel was beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance" (יְפַת-תֹּאַר וִיפַת מַרְאֶה). The verbal echo is precise and deliberate. Joseph, the son of Rachel, is described with his mother's own beauty phrase. The placement of this description at verse 6b — not at Joseph's first introduction but after seven verses establishing his managerial competence — is pointed: the narrator introduces Joseph's beauty precisely at the moment when it will become dangerous. Beauty is the trigger for what follows.

The LXX renders: καλὸς τῷ εἴδει καὶ ὡραῖος τῇ ὄψει ("handsome in form and fair in appearance") — using eidos (form/species) for to'ar and opsis (sight/appearance) for mareh. The choice of ōraios (ὡραῖος, literally "in season, blooming") for beautiful-of-appearance adds a nuance of youthful florescence not present in the Hebrew.

**v. 7 — "she lifted up her eyes toward Joseph" (וַתִּשָּׂא אֵשֶׁת-אֲדֹנָיו אֶת-עֵינֶיהָ)**
The idiom "to lift up the eyes" (nâsâ enayim, נָשָׂא עֵינַיִם) occurs throughout Genesis for the initiation of a gaze that leads to action: Abraham lifts his eyes and sees the three men (18:2); Lot lifts his eyes and sees the well-watered plain (13:10); Rebecca lifts her eyes and sees Isaac (24:64). Here Potiphar's wife lifts her eyes toward Joseph — her gaze is predatory. The verb nâsâ (נָשָׂא, "to lift, carry, bear") in this idiom suggests the deliberate elevation of the gaze, not an accidental glance. The command that follows — shikbah immi (שִׁכְבָה עִמִּי, "lie with me") — is blunt to the point of rudeness, two words in Hebrew with no social framing.

**v. 9 — chatati l'Elohim (וְחָטָאתִי לֵאלֹהִים) — "and sin against God"**
Joseph's refusal contains the first explicit articulation in the Joseph narrative (indeed, one of the clearest in all of Genesis) that sin is fundamentally an offense against God, not merely a social or ethical violation. The root chata' (חָטָא, "to miss the mark, to sin, to err") here takes the preposition le- (לְ, "to, toward, against"), constructing the offense as directed against God. Joseph frames adultery not as a violation of Potiphar's property rights (though that argument appears in vv. 8–9a) but as a sin against God — a theological claim that transcends the social framework.

The use of Elohim (אֱלֹהִים) rather than YHWH in Joseph's speech reflects the standard Joseph-narrative pattern: the characters use Elohim (the generic divine name), while the narrator uses YHWH. This phrase — "sin against God" — becomes thematically important in the later Joseph story: in 50:17 the brothers, fearful after Jacob's death, invoke Joseph's fear of God as they beg for forgiveness, using the same term Elohim. The echo is deliberate.

The LXX renders: ἁμαρτήσομαι ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ ("I shall sin before/in the presence of God") — using the future indicative and the preposition ἐναντίον ("before, in the presence of") rather than a simple dative, giving the sense of sinning in the sight of God — perhaps even slightly stronger than the MT's le-.

**v. 10 — "day after day" (יוֹם יוֹם)**
The distributive repetition yom yom (day after day / daily) is the Hebrew idiom for sustained, repeated action over time. The pressure Potiphar's wife applies is not a single incident but a campaign. The verse specifies two things Joseph refused: to lie beside her (shikbah etslah, לִשְׁכַּב אֶצְלָהּ — physical proximity) and to be with her (lihyot imah, לִהְיוֹת עִמָּהּ — sustained presence). The precision is legal and behavioral: Joseph refused both the act and the conditions that might lead to it. The doubling of refusal (not lying beside / not being with) shows the narrator's care in establishing Joseph's blamelessness beyond any ambiguity.

**v. 11 — "on such a day" (כְּהַיּוֹם הַזֶּה) — the day of the crisis**
The phrase kehavyom hazeh ("on such a day as this / on the day itself") is an idiom for a specific, remembered day as opposed to the daily pattern just described. The LXX and Vulgate both read "on a certain day" (τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ταύτῃ / die quadam). The phrase marks the break from the habitual (yom yom, v. 10) to the particular event. The detail that none of the household men were present inside is the only external circumstance that changes — the temptation was always there; now only the privacy is different. The narrator emphasizes Joseph's entry was for work (la'asot melakhto, "to do his work"), establishing innocent intent.

**v. 12 — beged (בֶּגֶד) — "garment" — the central symbol**
The noun beged (בֶּגֶד, "garment, covering") occurs seven times in this chapter (vv. 12, 13, 15, 16, 18 [×2], 19 — counting both v. 15 occurrences). The beged is the fulcrum of the accusation: seized (v. 12), retained (v. 16), displayed (vv. 13, 15, 18), and used as evidence (v. 19). This is the exact narrative structure of Genesis 37:31-33, where the ketonet passim (כְּתֹנֶת הַפַּסִּים, "the coat of passim") was dipped in blood, sent to Jacob, and Jacob was asked to "recognize" it as evidence. There the brothers used a garment as false evidence to destroy Jacob's understanding of Joseph's fate. Here Potiphar's wife uses a garment as false evidence to destroy Potiphar's confidence in Joseph.

The root beged also carries the connotation of treachery and betrayal (the verb bagad, בָּגַד, "to be treacherous, to act faithlessly") — an etymological resonance available to the Hebrew ear: the garment (beged) is itself a betrayal (begidah). This may be deliberate wordplay by the narrator.

**v. 14 — ish ivri (אִישׁ עִבְרִי) — "a Hebrew man"**
The term ivri (עִבְרִי, "Hebrew") is used throughout Genesis as an ethnic/social marker, often by or for non-Israelites speaking about Israel (cf. 14:13, where Abram is "Abram the Hebrew"). Here Potiphar's wife uses it twice (v. 14 to the servants; v. 17 to Potiphar, where she says "the Hebrew slave") to distance Joseph from the household and to leverage xenophobia in her accusation. The shift from "ish ivri" (v. 14 — "a Hebrew man") to "ha'eved ha'ivri" (v. 17 — "the Hebrew slave") marks an escalation: to the servants she names his ethnicity; to her husband she adds his servile status. The LXX renders ἄνθρωπον Εβραῖον ("a Hebrew man") and τὸν παῖδα τὸν Εβραῖον ("the Hebrew boy/servant") — paida (παῖς) carrying the ambiguity of "child/slave/servant."

**v. 14 — letsachek banu (לְצַחֶק בָּנוּ) — "to make sport of us"**
The verb tsachaq (צָחַק, Pi'el: lesachek) here means "to mock, to make sport of, to ridicule." The same root is used in 21:9 (Ishmael "making sport of" Isaac — the incident that led to Hagar's expulsion), in 26:8 (Isaac "laughing/fondling" Rebecca — where the context is clearly sexual play), and notably in Exodus 32:6 (Israel "rose up to play/make sport" before the golden calf, in a context the rabbis read as orgiastic). The range of the root tsachaq covers playful laughter, sexual play, and mockery. Potiphar's wife deploys it ambiguously: to the servants she presents it as an insult ("to make sport of us / to mock us"); to Potiphar she will say "to make sport of me" (bi, in v. 17). The accusation is of sexual assault on the one hand and ethnic contempt on the other — a two-level charge.

**v. 19 — vayichar appo (וַיִּחַר אַפּוֹ) — "and his anger burned"**
The idiom charah aph (חָרָה אַף, literally "the nose burned/was hot") is the standard biblical expression for anger: the nose reddens and flares with rage. The referent of "his anger" is grammatically ambiguous — it is most naturally Potiphar's anger. The object of the anger is not explicitly stated — it could be anger at Joseph (the expected reading given the imprisonment that follows) or, in some interpretive traditions, anger at his wife (for allowing the situation). The rabbis (e.g., Rashi following earlier midrash) suggest that Potiphar knew his wife's account was false but was constrained by honor to act — hence his relatively mild response of imprisonment rather than execution (the normal penalty for a slave who assaulted a master's wife). The LXX renders: ὠργίσθη δὲ Ιωσηφ ὁ κύριος ("and the master of Joseph was angry") — using orgizō (ὀργίζω, "to be angry") and specifying "the master of Joseph" as the subject.

**v. 20 — beit haSohar (בֵּית הַסֹּהַר) — "the prison-house"**
The term sohar (סֹהַר) is a hapax legomenon in this specific form — it appears only in Genesis 39–40 (vv. 20, 21, 22, 23; 40:3, 5). The root may be related to the Aramaic or may derive from a term meaning "round enclosure" (cf. Aramaic sahar, "moon" — i.e., a round tower or enclosure). The LXX renders oxyrunchos / to oxyrunkhon or simply the prison — but in Vaticanus the rendering is ὀχύρωμα (ochyrōma, "stronghold, fortress"). Modern scholars generally render "prison" or "dungeon." The repeated phrase beit haSohar ("house of the prison / prison-house") — four times in this chapter alone — becomes the spatial marker of Joseph's lowest point, from which the divine reversal will come.

**v. 21 — vayet elav chesed (וַיֵּט אֵלָיו חֶסֶד) — "he extended covenant-faithfulness to him"**
The verb natah (נָטָה, Hiphil: vayet) means "to stretch out, to extend, to incline." In collocation with chesed (חֶסֶד) the phrase means "to show/extend steadfast love." Chesed is the richest word in the chapter: it is covenant faithfulness, steadfast love, loyal-kindness — the word that describes the LORD's characterological commitment to those in covenant relationship (cf. Exodus 34:6, where chesed is the heart of the divine self-revelation). The fact that YHWH is said to "extend chesed" to Joseph in a prison — the most abased position a free man could occupy short of death — is the theological centerpiece of the chapter. The same theological claim will be made of Ruth (2:20) and is embedded in the Psalms' affirmations of divine faithfulness in suffering (cf. Ps 136, where chesed appears in every verse; Ps 107:15-16 for the prison image specifically).

The LXX renders: ἔδωκεν αὐτῷ χάριν ("he gave grace/favor to him") — using charis (χάρις), which captures the relational gift dimension of chesed but loses its covenantal weight. The same word charis appears in the prior verse-clause for "favor in the eyes of" (hanan/chen — חֵן), which the LXX also renders charis — flattening the distinction between chen (grace/favor — a one-time gift) and chesed (covenant faithfulness — a characterological commitment). This LXX conflation is one of the most theologically significant differences in the chapter.

**v. 23 — structural inclusio with vv. 2–3**
The chapter closes with the same vocabulary it opened with. Compare:
- v. 2: "the LORD was with Joseph, and he was a prosperous man / matsliach"
- v. 3: "his master saw that the LORD was with him and everything he did the LORD made prosper / matzliach in his hand"
- v. 23: "the LORD was with him and what he did the LORD made prosper / matzliach"

The triple use of matsliach/matzliach (Hiphil of tsalach) forms the chapter's spine. The descent of 39:1 (hurad) is answered by the ascent-in-place of 39:23 (matzliach): even in the lowest place, YHWH's blessing makes Joseph rise. This prepares the reader for the two-stage ascent of chapters 40-41: from prison to Pharaoh's court.

---

### Structural Notes: The Garment Parallel and the Descent Motif

**Two Garments, Two False Accusations — The Structural Mirror**

Genesis 39 is the second act in a three-act drama of false evidence. The structural parallel between chapter 37 and chapter 39 is one of the most precisely crafted literary designs in the Hebrew Bible:

| Element | Genesis 37 | Genesis 39 |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Garment | ketonet passim (כְּתֹנֶת פַּסִּים) — coat of passim | beged (בֶּגֶד) — garment |
| Evidence produced | "recognize, please" — is this your son's coat? | garment left in her hand |
| Accusation | brothers to father Jacob | Potiphar's wife to Potiphar |
| False charge | Joseph is dead / torn by beast | Joseph assaulted his master's wife |
| Result | Jacob mourns; Joseph separated from family | Joseph imprisoned; separated from Potiphar's household |
| Who is deceived | Jacob (father) | Potiphar (master) |
| Instigator(s) | Joseph's brothers | Potiphar's wife |
| Joseph's agency | passive victim | passive victim (he fled) |
| Divine response | LORD absent from scene | "the LORD was with Joseph" (v. 21) |

The double garment-as-false-evidence structure is not accidental but the narrator's deliberate shaping of the Joseph cycle. Joseph is twice falsely accused by means of a garment left behind — once the garment his father gave him (ketonet passim, the mark of favor), once the garment he himself wore as a free man in Potiphar's house (beged). Each garment represents a status he loses: beloved son, trusted overseer. Each false accusation strips him of that status. The trajectory of descent is: beloved son → slave → prisoner.

The word beged itself, as noted above, resonates with the verb bagad ("to betray"). In chapter 37 the betrayal is literal (his brothers betray him). In chapter 39 the betrayal is performed through the beged — the garment stands in for the treacherous act.

**The Descent Motif (Yarad / Hurad)**

The verb yarad (ירד, "to go down") is the structural marker of the Joseph narrative's first movement:

- **37:25** — the Ishmaelites were coming down (hored, Hiphil) from Gilead to Egypt
- **37:35** — Jacob refused to go down (larid) in comfort to Sheol
- **37:36** — "and Joseph was brought down [hurad] to Egypt" (chapter-ending verse)
- **38:1** — "and Judah went down [vayyered] from his brothers" (chapter-opening verse — the Judah interlude)
- **39:1** — "and Joseph was brought down [hurad] to Egypt" (resuming 37:36 with the identical clause)

The verbal repetition of hurad in 37:36 and 39:1 functions as an inclusio around the Judah parenthesis of chapter 38 — the narrator has Joseph descend at the end of chapter 37 and descends him again at the beginning of chapter 39, making the frame explicit. Within chapter 39 itself the descent continues: from Potiphar's household to the prison-house (beit haSohar, the lowest spatial point in the narrative). The movement of descent (yarad) will be reversed in 41:14 when Joseph is brought up (vayyirtsuhu, "they brought him up quickly") from the pit-prison to Pharaoh — an ascent (alah) that reverses every descent that preceded it.

**The "LORD with Joseph" Formula as Counter-Descent**

Against the downward trajectory of geography and social status, the narrator inserts the divine-presence formula: "the LORD was with Joseph" (vv. 2, 21). This formula appears only when Joseph is at a nadir:
- v. 2: in Potiphar's house as a slave (first descent completed)
- v. 21: in the prison-house as a prisoner (second descent completed)

The pattern suggests that the divine presence formula functions as a theological inversion of descent: the lower Joseph goes, the more explicitly the narrator asserts YHWH's accompanying presence. This is the narrative strategy of Psalm 23 ("though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death") and Psalm 139:8 ("if I make my bed in Sheol, you are there") — divine presence is not defeated by depth.

---

### LXX Variant Table — Genesis 39 (MT vs. Vaticanus)

| Verse | MT (Hebrew) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Notes |
|-------|------------|-----------------|-------|
| 39:1 | וְיוֹסֵף הוּרַד מִצְרָיְמָה — "and Joseph was brought down to Egypt" | Ιωσηφ δὲ κατήχθη εἰς Αἴγυπτον — "And Joseph was brought down into Egypt" | LXX accurately renders the Hophal passive with aorist passive κατήχθη. |
| 39:1 | סְרִיס פַּרְעֹה — "court official/eunuch of Pharaoh" | σπάδοντι Φαραω — "eunuch of Pharaoh" | LXX chooses the physiological meaning of seris (σπάδων = castrated male), creating tension with the fact that Potiphar is married. MT is ambiguous. |
| 39:1 | שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים — "chief of the guard/slaughterers" | ἀρχιμαγείρῳ — "chief cook" | LXX renders literally (μάγειρος = cook/butcher), missing the administrative-military sense. |
| 39:2 | אִישׁ מַצְלִיחַ — "a prosperous/successful man" | ἦν ἐν τῷ οἴκῳ τοῦ κυρίου — "was in the house of his master" | LXX appears to omit or rephrase the "prosperous man" clause, folding it into context. The matsliach emphasis (three-fold in MT) is less prominent in LXX. |
| 39:6 | יְפֵה-תֹאַר וִיפֵה מַרְאֶה — "beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance" | καλὸς τῷ εἴδει καὶ ὡραῖος τῇ ὄψει — "handsome in form and fair/blooming in appearance" | LXX adds ὡραῖος ("blooming, in season") for the second yefeh — a slight expansion conveying youthful beauty. Matches the identical phrase for Rachel (29:17). |
| 39:9 | וְחָטָאתִי לֵאלֹהִים — "and sin against God" | ἁμαρτήσομαι ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ — "I shall sin before/in the presence of God" | LXX uses ἐναντίον ("before, in the face of") rather than a simple dative, giving a slightly stronger sense of visibility before God. Future indicative vs. MT's waw-consecutive imperfect. |
| 39:14 | אִישׁ עִבְרִי — "a Hebrew man" | ἄνθρωπον Εβραῖον — "a Hebrew man" | LXX closely follows MT. |
| 39:14 | לְצַחֶק בָּנוּ — "to make sport of us / to mock us" | παίζειν μεθ᾽ ἡμῶν — "to play with us" | LXX uses paizō (παίζω, "to play, to sport") — the same root used in the Exodus 32:6 LXX for the golden calf incident — preserving the ambiguity of mock/play/sexual sport. |
| 39:17 | הָעֶבֶד הָעִבְרִי — "the Hebrew slave" | τὸν παῖδα τὸν Εβραῖον — "the Hebrew boy/servant" | LXX uses pais (παῖς), which ranges from "child" to "slave/servant." Slightly softened from eved (עֶבֶד, slave). |
| 39:20 | בֵּית הַסֹּהַר — "the prison-house" | εἰς τὸ ὀχύρωμα — "into the stronghold/fortress" | LXX renders ochyrōma (ὀχύρωμα, "stronghold, fortified place") rather than a term for prison, suggesting a fortified detention facility. Closer to a citadel than a dungeon. |
| 39:21 | וַיֵּט אֵלָיו חֶסֶד — "and he extended covenant-faithfulness to him" | ἔδωκεν αὐτῷ χάριν — "and he gave grace/favor to him" | Significant: LXX uses charis (χάρις) for both chesed (covenant-faithfulness) and chen (grace/favor, v. 21b), collapsing an important theological distinction. Chesed is a covenantal commitment; chen is situational favor. LXX flattens both to charis. |
| 39:23 | יְהוָה מַצְלִיחַ — "the LORD was making prosper" | κύριος μετ᾽ αὐτοῦ ἦν — "the Lord was with him" | LXX at v. 23 renders the second clause as "the Lord was with him" (resuming the presence formula) rather than explicitly rendering matzliach ("was making prosper"). The MT's closing triple use of the matsliach root is slightly compressed in LXX. |

### Key Term Lexicon for Genesis 39

| Hebrew | Transliteration | Gloss | Notes |
|--------|----------------|-------|-------|
| הוּרַד | hurad | "was brought down" | Hophal passive of yarad (ירד); most passive descent form |
| סְרִיס | seris | "court official" | Can mean eunuch; here "official" preferred given marital context |
| שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים | sar hatabachim | "chief of the guard" | Lit. "chief of the slaughterers" |
| מַצְלִיחַ | matsliach | "prospering/successful" | Hiphil ptcp. of tsalach; three-fold structural marker |
| יְפֵה-תֹאַר וִיפֵה מַרְאֶה | yefeh-toar vi-yefeh mareh | "beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance" | Identical phrase to Rachel (29:17); beauty as danger |
| שִׁכְבָה עִמִּי | shikbah immi | "lie with me" | Blunt imperative; two words in Hebrew |
| חָטָאתִי לֵאלֹהִים | chatati l'Elohim | "sin against God" | First explicit theological articulation of sin |
| יוֹם יוֹם | yom yom | "day after day" | Distributive repetition = sustained habitual action |
| בֶּגֶד | beged | "garment" | Seven occurrences; resonates with bagad (betrayal) |
| אִישׁ עִבְרִי | ish ivri | "a Hebrew man" | Ethnic marker; repeated in v. 17 as "the Hebrew slave" |
| לְצַחֶק | letsachek | "to make sport of" | Pi'el of tsachaq; range: mock/play/sexual sport |
| בֵּית הַסֹּהַר | beit haSohar | "the prison-house" | Hapax form of sohar; spatial marker of lowest point |
| וַיֵּט אֵלָיו חֶסֶד | vayet elav chesed | "extended covenant-faithfulness to him" | Central theological claim; chesed in the prison |
